one_line_takeaway: Training MIA shadow models by knowledge-distilling the target model (best with a direct MSE logit-matching loss) lifts offline likelihood-ratio attacks in the low-FPR regime and, unlike prior LiRA, works even when the attacker does not know the target's architecture.
key_finding: Distillation-guided shadow training improves the low-FPR regime that matters for MIA, and does so even when the attacker does not know the target architecture. GLiRA (MSE) consistently outperforms the baseline LiRA across all FPR values in all setups. In the same-architecture ResNet-34 setting (Table I), GLiRA (MSE) reaches TPR@0.01%FPR of 2.62% on CIFAR10 vs LiRA's 0.37%, and TPR@1%FPR of 49.41% on CIFAR100 vs LiRA's 29.80%. In the true black-box setting (Table II, target ResNet-34 / shadow VGG16), GLiRA (MSE) reaches 45.67% TPR@1%FPR on CIFAR100 vs LiRA's 25.80%. GLiRA (KL) dominates the high-FPR regime and AUC (e.g., 0.925 AUC on CIFAR100 in Table I, ~10% over Canary and ~15% over LiRA) but is unstable at low FPR. The authors fix alpha=1.0 (pure distillation loss most helps the attack) and prefer tau=1.0/MSE because large tau introduces a logit-mean-deviation term (Eq. 14-15) that hurts logit matching.
core_contribution: The paper proposes GLiRA (Guided Likelihood Ratio Attack), a membership inference attack (MIA) that trains the attacker's shadow models via knowledge distillation of the target model rather than by ordinary supervised training. The authors' own insight is that forcing each shadow model to mimic the target's outputs (treating the target as 'teacher' and each shadow model as 'student') yields shadow models whose output distributions align more closely with the target's, which sharpens the offline likelihood-ratio test at low false-positive rates. Crucially, this alignment is achieved without knowing the target's architecture, making the offline LiRA of Carlini et al. usable in a true black-box setting where architecture mismatch would otherwise cause the shadow-model output distribution to diverge from the target's (motivated in the paper's Fig. 1). The paper contributes two variants: GLiRA (KL) using a KL-divergence distillation loss (alpha=1.0, tau=1.0) and GLiRA (MSE) using a mean-squared-error logit-matching loss (alpha=1.0), the latter adapted from Kim et al.'s KD analysis to force direct logit matching. It also shows the MSE variant's needed target logits can be reconstructed from output probabilities up to a constant, assuming the logit sum is near zero.
threat_model: Black-box membership inference following the standard Carlini et al. membership-inference game (challenger trains target ft on Dt sampled from distribution D; adversary must decide whether a queried point (x*,y*) was in Dt). The adversary has (1) query access to the target model returning a continuous vector of class probabilities (or logits), and (2) access to the underlying training-data distribution D to sample data for shadow-model training. The adversary does NOT need the target model's architecture nor its exact training dataset. The paper works in the OFFLINE LiRA setting (only OUT shadow models trained, no per-query IN models), so the attack pipeline is built once. A weaker variant (Sec. VII-C) covers the case where only probabilities (not logits) are exposed, reconstructing logits via zk = ln(pk) - (1/K) sum_j ln(pj).
limitations: (1) Scope limited to image-classification models on small datasets (CIFAR10, CIFAR100, CINIC10); no language, tabular, or large-scale models. (2) High computational cost: 128 distilled shadow models per experiment; reducing this is left to future work. (3) Distribution shift between target and shadow data noticeably degrades the attack (Sec. VII-B / Fig. 7). (4) GLiRA (KL) gives unstable/uneven low-FPR performance (e.g., poor on CINIC10 WideResNet28-10), so no single variant wins everywhere. (5) The MSE variant needs target logits; when only probabilities are exposed, logits are only reconstructed up to a constant under a near-zero-logit-sum assumption (small ~0.012 error observed, but an assumption nonetheless). (6) The Trajectory baseline could only be compared on CINIC10 because its tiny-target-set setup is infeasible to reproduce at the paper's 20k target-set size, weakening that comparison. (7) Gains over LiRA in the same-architecture setting partly reflect the well-known LiRA-preferred regime; the more novel claim is the black-box (mismatched-architecture) result.

datasets_used: CINIC10 (with the CIFAR10 portion removed for training)
quantitative_results: Table I (target ResNet-34, shadow ResNet-34): GLiRA (MSE) TPR@0.01%FPR CIFAR10 = 2.62% vs LiRA 0.37%, Calibration 0.37%, Canary 0.04%
Table I: GLiRA (MSE) TPR@1%FPR CIFAR100 = 49.41% vs LiRA 29.80%, Canary 33.45%
Table I: GLiRA (KL) AUC CIFAR100 = 0.925 vs LiRA 0.787, Canary 0.827, Calibration 0.779
Table II (target ResNet-34, shadow VGG16, mismatched architecture): GLiRA (MSE) TPR@1%FPR CIFAR100 = 45.67% vs LiRA 25.80%, Canary 27.76%
Table II: GLiRA (KL) AUC CIFAR100 = 0.916 vs LiRA 0.733
Table II: GLiRA (MSE) TPR@0.01%FPR CIFAR10 = 1.63% vs LiRA 0.48%
Reported same-architecture improvements on CIFAR100: +7% TPR@0.1%FPR and +16% TPR@1%FPR over baseline; mismatched-architecture: +7.5% TPR@0.1%FPR and +17% TPR@1%FPR
Model clean-classification accuracy: 87.0-91.0% (CIFAR10), 57.27-66.7% (CIFAR100), 78.0-82.0% (CINIC10) depending on architecture
Logit-reconstruction error under near-zero logit-sum assumption ~0.012 absolute (VGG16 largest average logit sum 0.12 on CIFAR10)
baselines_compared: LiRA - Likelihood Ratio Attack, Carlini et al. 2022 [9] (the offline variant, primary baseline)
Difficulty Calibration MIA, Watson et al. 2022 [13]
Canary in a Coalmine, Wen et al. 2023 [10] (offline variant)
Loss-Trajectory MIA, Liu et al. 2022 [11] (compared only on CINIC10)
